children.text: A manoeuvre in which a turn is made away from a designated track followed by a turn in the opposite direction to permit the aircraft to intercept and proceed along the reciprocal of the designated track is called a:

You are on an IFR flight executing a circling approach. A descend below the MDA should not be made until: 1. the pilot has the landing threshold in sight 2. visual reference has been established and can be maintained 3. the required obstacle clearance can be maintained and a landing can be made The combination regrouping all the correct answers is:
In an approach procedure, a descent or climb conducted in a holding pattern is called:
Where an operational advantage can be obtained, an ILS procedure may include a dead reckoning segment from a fix to the localizer. The DR track will:

answers: Intersect the localizer at 30° and will not be more 10 NM in length
Intersect the localizer at 30° and will not be more 5 NM in length
Intersect the localizer at 45° and will not be more 5 NM in length
Intersect the localizer at 45° and will not be more 10 NM in length
correctAnswer: Intersect the localizer at 45° and will not be more 10 NM in length

children.text: Which is the obstacle clearance in the primary area of the intermediate approach segment in an instrument approach procedure?
In an instrument approach procedure, the segment in which aligment and descent for landing are made is called:
In a precision approach (ILS), the final approach segment begins at the:
In a precision approach (ILS), generally glide path intersection occurs at heights above runway elevation from:
In an offset entry into an omnidirectional racetrack procedure, the time on the 30° offset track is limited to:
How many separate segments has an instrument approach procedure?
Where does the initial approach segment in an instrument approach procedure commence?
Which is the obstacle clearance in the primary area of the initial approach segment in an instrument approach procedure?
In a procedure turn (45°/180°), a 45° turn away from the outbound track is performed from the start of turn for categories A and B aircraft for:
In a procedure turn (45°/180°), a 45° turn away from the outbound track is performed from the start of the turn for categories C, D, E aircraft for:
In a precision approach (ILS), obstacle clearance surfaces assume that the pilot does not normally deviate from the centreline, after being established on track, more than:

answers: One and a half of scale deflection

children.text: In a precision approach (ILS), the OCA or OCH values are based among other standard conditions, on a vertical distance between the flight paths of the wheels and glide path antenna, not greater than:
Which are the phases of a missed approach procedure?
Normally missed approach procedures are based on a nominal missed approach climb gradient of:
Where does the initial phase of a missed approach procedure end?

answers: At the point where the climb is established
At the point where a new approach, holding or return to en-route flight is initiated
At the first point where 50m (164 ft) obstacle clearance is obtained and can be maintained
correctAnswer: At the point where the climb is established

children.text: The term used to discribe the visual phase of flight after completing an instrument approach, to bring an aircraft into position for landing on runway which is not suitably located for straigh-in approach, is:

It is permissible to eliminate from consideration a particular sector where a prominent obstacle exists in the visual manoeuvering (circling) area outside the final approach and missed approach area. When this option is exercised, the published procedure:

answers: Prohibits circling within the total sector in which the obstacle exists
Recommends not to perform circling within the total sector in which the obstacle exists
Prohibits the circling approach to the affected runway
correctAnswer: Prohibits circling within the total sector in which the obstacle exists

children.text: When the visual manoeuvring (circling) area has been established the obstacle clearance altitude/height (OCA/H) is determined:

answers: For all categories of aircraft, and it is the same for all of them
For each category of aircraft, and it may be different for each one of them
Only for categories A and B aircraft
Only for categories C, D and E aircraft
correctAnswer: For each category of aircraft, and it may be different for each one of them

children.text: A circling approach is:

answers: A visual flight manoeuvre keeping the runway in sight
A visual manoeuvre to be conducted only in IMc
A flight manoeuvre to be performed only under radar vectoring
correctAnswer: A visual flight manoeuvre keeping the runway in sight

children.text: If visual reference is lost while circling to land from an instrument approach, it is expected that the pilot will make an initial climbing turn towards the:
Obstacle clearance for an ILS approach is based on the assumption that the pilot does not deviate from the centre line more than:

answers: full scale deflection of the localizer indicator and half scale deflection of the glidepath indicator
half scale deflection of the localizer indicator
full scale deflection of the localizer indicator
half scale deflection of the glidepath indicator and horizontal 35 ° off the centerline
correctAnswer: half scale deflection of the localizer indicator

children.text: Who establishes the OCA/H (Obstacle Clearance Altitude/Height) for an approach procedure?
On a non-precision approach a so-called "straight-in-approach" is considered acceptable, if the angle between the final approach track and the runway centreline is:
Normally, the maximum descent gradient, applicable in the final approach segment to ensure the required minimum obstacle clearance, is:
The primary area of an instrument approach segment is:

answers: A defined area symmetrically disposed about the nominal flight track in which full obstacle clearance is provided
the most critical part of the segment where the minimum altitude should be kept very carefully
the outside part of the segment where the obstacle clearance increases from o ft to the appropriate minimum
the first part of the segment
correctAnswer: A defined area symmetrically disposed about the nominal flight track in which full obstacle clearance is provided

children.text: In the primary area, the obstacle clearance for the initial approach segment provides at least:
A "precision approach" is a direct instrument approach...

answers: using at least one source of bearing information and one source of elevation or distance information
using bearing, elevation and distance information
using bearing, elevation and distance information, providing the pilot uses a flight director or an autopilot certified to a height below 200 ft
carried out by a crew of at least two pilots trained with a specific working method
correctAnswer: using bearing, elevation and distance information

children.text: Unless otherwise indicated, the missed approach procedures published on the IAC charts are based on a minimum climb gradient of:
Minimum sector altitudes are determined by the inbound radial in relation to the IAF. These sectors are established for a distance from the IAF of:
The width of the corridor around a specified arrival route is:
In general, during a straight-in approach, the MDH cannot be below:
For a category I precision approach, the decision height cannot be lower than:
(IR) Minimum sector altitudes published on approach charts provide at least the following obstacle clearance:

answers: 300 m within 25 km of the homing facility associated with the approach procedure for that aerodrome
300 m within 25 nm of the homing facility associated with the approach procedure for that aerodrome
300 m within 25 nm of the initial approach fix
300 m within 25 km of the FAF or FAP
correctAnswer: 300 m within 25 nm of the homing facility associated with the approach procedure for that aerodrome

children.text: (IR) For the construction of precision approaches, which is the operationally preferred glide path angle?
(IR) Which is the minimum obstacle clearance within the primary area of an intermediate approach segment?

(IR) During a visual circling, descent below MDA/H shall not be made until: A) Visual reference has been established and can be maintained B) The pilot has the landing threshold in sight. C) The required obstacle clearance can be maintained and the aircraft is in a position to carry out a landing. D) A landing clearance has been received by ATc
(IR) If, during a visual circling, visual reference is lost while circling to land from an instrument approach, the pilot shall:

answers: Climb straight ahead to the minimum sector altitude
Require immediate assistance from ATC which is obliged to provide radar vectors in order to maintain obstacle clearance
Descend further in order to reach an altitude where visual reference can be maintained
Make an initial climbing turn towards the landing runway and follow the missed approach procedures
correctAnswer: Make an initial climbing turn towards the landing runway and follow the missed approach procedures

children.text: (IR) Based on operational considerations, a margin may be added to the OCA of a non-precision approach. The result is then called:
(IR) Where does the initial section of a missed approach procedure end?

answers: When enroute either to hold or departure
When a height of 50 m has been achieved and maintained

children.text: (IR) Where a final approach fix (FAF) is specified for a non-precision approach procedure, what is the minimum obstacle clearance fixed margin that is applied for all aircraft?
(IR) For non precision approaches, the Final Approach Fix (FAF) is set at a specified distance from the threshold of the instrument runway. What is the maximum this distance can be?
(IR) Under what circumstances would an ILS glide path in excess of 3° be used?

answers: Where other means of obstacle clearance are impracticable
Where noise abatement restrictions make a less steep approach impracticable
correctAnswer: Where other means of obstacle clearance are impracticable

children.text: (IR) Where does the missed approach procedure start?

answers: Over the threshold of the instrument runway
At any point that the criteria to continue the approach is lost

children.text: (IR) If the ILS glide path transmitter fails during an instrument approach procedure, can the approach be continued?

answers: Yes, but the DH becomes a MDH
Yes, if the flight is in VMc
It depends if the descent on the glide path has already been initiated
Yes, but the non-precision criteria must be complied with
correctAnswer: Yes, but the non-precision criteria must be complied with

children.text: (IR) Why is the descent gradient kept as low as possible in the intermediate approach segment?

answers: Usually track guidance is poor in this segment resulting in a requirement for a high MOc
Pilots cannot cope with track maintenance an a high rate of descent
It is not possible to guarantee full obstacle clearance in this segment
This is the segment in which speed and configuration are adjusted
correctAnswer: This is the segment in which speed and configuration are adjusted

children.text: (IR) A Visual Manoeuvring (Circling) Area may be sectored to exclude a prominent obstacle. Under what circumstances is this permitted?

answers: The obstacle lies on the other side of the aerodrome from the threshold of the landing runway
The obstacle is outside the final approach or missed approach areas
Flight visibility of 1500 m or more and cloud ceiling of 850 ft or more
The obstacle height for that obstacle is not more than 10% higher than the OCH calculated without consideration of that obstacle
correctAnswer: The obstacle is outside the final approach or missed approach areas

children.text: (IR) Where does the final approach segment for a precision approach start?
What will be your action if you can not comply with a standard holding pattern?

answers: a non-standard holding pattern is permitted
it is permitted to deviate from the prescribed holding pattern at pilots discretion
Follow the radio communication failure procedure
inform the ATC immediately and request a revised clearance
correctAnswer: inform the ATC immediately and request a revised clearance

children.text: In a holding pattern all turns are to be made at a:

answers: rate of 3°per second or at a bank angle of 20°, which ever requires the lesser bank
rate of 3°per second or at a bank angle of 25°, which ever requires the lesser bank
correctAnswer: rate of 3°per second or at a bank angle of 25°, which ever requires the lesser bank

children.text: Entering a holding pattern at FL 110 with a jet aircraft, which will be the maximum speed?
Unless otherwise published or instructed by ATC, all turns after initial entry into the holding pattern shall be made into which direction?

answers: First right and then to the left
Teardrop to the left and then to the right

children.text: What is the outbound timing in a holding pattern up to FL 140?

You have received holding instructions for a radio fix. The published holding procedure is: all turns to the right, 1 minute outbound, inbound MC 052°. You are approaching the fix an inbound Magnetic Track 232°. Select the available entry procedure.
What is the outbound timing in a holding pattern above FL 140?
In relation to the three entry sectors, the entry into the holding pattern shall be according to:
Related to the three entry sectors in a holding pattern, there is a zone of flexibility on either side of the sectors boundaries of:
How far beyond the boundary of the holding area extends the buffer area?
In a standard holding pattern turns are made:

answers: in a direction depending on the wind direction
in a direction depending on the entry

children.text: (IR) The entry into a holding pattern shall be according to:

answers: Magnetic heading in relation to the three entry sectors
Magnetic track in relation to the three entry sectors
True track in relation to the three entry sectors
True heading in relation to the three entry sectors
correctAnswer: Magnetic heading in relation to the three entry sectors

children.text: (IR) In a holding pattern, turns are to be made:

answers: At a bank angle of 20° or at a rate of 3° per second, whichever requires the lesser bank
At a bank angle of 15° or at a rate of 3° per second, whichever requires the lesser bank
At a bank angle of 30° or at a rate of 3° per second, whichever requires the lesser bank
At a bank angle of 25° or at a rate of 3° per second, whichever requires the lesser bank
correctAnswer: At a bank angle of 25° or at a rate of 3° per second, whichever requires the lesser bank

children.text: (IR) Which is the normal still air outbound time in a holding pattern?

answers: One and one half minute for altitudes above 10'000 ft
None of the above is correct
One and one half minute for altitudes above 14'000 ft
One minute for altitudes above 14'000 ft
correctAnswer: One and one half minute for altitudes above 14'000 ft

children.text: (IR) Is the length of the outbound leg of a holding pattern always expressed in terms of time?

answers: No, where G/S is less than 65 kt, the outbound leg must be at least 2 nm long
Yes even where DME is used, the maximum length is always in time
No, where DME is used it may be specified in terms of distance
correctAnswer: No, where DME is used it may be specified in terms of distance

children.text: The pilot of a departing aircraft flying under IFR shall change the altimeter setting from QNH to standard setting 1013.25 hPa when passing:
The transition altitude of an aerodrome should not be below:
The vertical position of an aircraft at or below the transition altitude will be reported:
The vertical position of an aircraft at or above the transition level will be reported:
Transition from altitude to flight level, and vice-versa is done:

answers: at transition altitude during climb and transition level during descent
at transition level during climb and transition altitude during descent
correctAnswer: at transition altitude during climb and transition level during descent

children.text: What will be the transponder mode and code for radio communication failure?
When an aircraft carries a serviceable transponder, the pilot shall operate the transponder:

answers: At all times during flight, regardless of whether the aircraft is within or outside airspace where SSR is used for ATS purposes
Only when the aircraft is flying within controlled airspace
Only when the aircraft is flying within airspace where SSR is used for ATS purposes
correctAnswer: At all times during flight, regardless of whether the aircraft is within or outside airspace where SSR is used for ATS purposes

children.text: When the aircraft carries serviceable Mode C equipment, the pilot:

answers: Shall continuously operate this mode regardless of ATC instructions
Shall continuously operate this mode unless otherwise directed by ATc
Shall continuously operate this mode only when the aircraft is within controlled airspace
Shall continuously operate this mode only when directed by ATc
correctAnswer: Shall continuously operate this mode unless otherwise directed by ATc

children.text: The pilot of an aircaft losing two-way communications shall set the transponder to Mode A Code:
When an aircraft is subjected to unlawful interference, the pilot-in-command shall indicate the situation by setting the transponder to:
Pilots shall not operate the SSR special position indicator (IDENT) feature unless:

answers: They operate a transponder with Mode c
They operate within non controlled airspace

children.text: When acknowledging mode/code setting instructions, pilots shall:

answers: Read back the mode and code to be set
Read back only the code to be set
correctAnswer: Read back the mode and code to be set

children.text: Your transponder code assigned by ATC is 5320. In flight, in case of radio communications failure, you will squawk code:
A controlled airspace extending upwards from the surface of the earth to a specified upper limit is:
ATS airspaces where IFR and VFR flights are permited, all flights are subject to air traffic control service and are separated from each other are classified as:
Aerodrome traffic is:

answers: All traffic in the aerodrome circuit
All traffic on the movement area and flying in the vicinity of an aerodrome
All traffic on the manoeuvring area
All traffic on the manoeuvring area and flying in the vicinity of an aerodrome
correctAnswer: All traffic on the manoeuvring area and flying in the vicinity of an aerodrome

children.text: Air Traffic Service unit means:

answers: Air Traffic Control units and Flight Information Centers
Air Traffic Control units, Flight Information Centers or Air Services reporting offices
Air Traffic Control units and Air Services reporting offices
Flight Information Centers and Air Services reporting offices
correctAnswer: Air Traffic Control units, Flight Information Centers or Air Services reporting offices

children.text: Regarding Aerodrome Flight Information (AFIS):

answers: its purpose is to supply ATC services but it is not a state organisation
it has the same privileges and prerogatives as an ATC organisation but its activity is neither continuous nor regular
its only purpose is to relay ATC information to the aircraft in flight or on the ground
it can only supply limited services to the users and under no circumstances may it supply ATC services
correctAnswer: it can only supply limited services to the users and under no circumstances may it supply ATC services

children.text: Which condition is requested so that an aerodrome may be considered controlled?

answers: The aerodrome shall be provided with a Control Tower
The aerodrome shall be located within a Control Zone
The aerodrome shall be located within a Control Zone (CTR) and provided with a Control Tower
The aerodrome shall be located within a controlled airspace
correctAnswer: The aerodrome shall be provided with a Control Tower

children.text: Flight Information Region (FIR) is an airspace within which the following services are provided:

answers: Flight Information Service and Alerting Service
Flight Information Service, Alerting Service and Advisory Service
Flight Information Service and Advisory Service
correctAnswer: Flight Information Service and Alerting Service

children.text: Control Area (CTA) is defined as follows:

answers: A controlled airspace extending upwards from a height of 1000 feet above the earth
A controlled airspace extending upwards from a specified limit above the earth
A controlled airspace extending upwards from the surface of the earth to a specified limit
A controlled airspace extending upwards from a height of 900 feet above the earth
correctAnswer: A controlled airspace extending upwards from a specified limit above the earth

children.text: A Control Zone shall extend laterally to at least:

answers: 5 nautical miles from the centre of the aerodrome or aerodromes concerned in the direction from which approaches may be made
10 miles from the centre of the aerodrome or aerodromes concerned in the directionfrom which approaches may be made
20 miles from the centre of the aerodrome or aerodromes concerned in the direction from which approaches may be made
15 miles from the centre of the aerodrome or aerodromes concerned in the direction from which approaches may be made
correctAnswer: 5 nautical miles from the centre of the aerodrome or aerodromes concerned in the direction from which approaches may be made

children.text: A lower limit of a Control Area shall be established at a height above the ground level or water of not less than:
The units providing Air Traffic Services are:

answers: Area Control Centre - Flight Information Region - Approach Control Office and Tower
Area Control Centre - Approach Control Office and Aerodrome Control Tower
Area Control Centre - Advisory Centre - Flight Information Centre - Approach Control Office and Tower
Area Control Centre - Flight Information Centre - Approach Control Office - Aerodrome Control Tower and Air Traffic Services reporting office
correctAnswer: Area Control Centre - Flight Information Centre - Approach Control Office - Aerodrome Control Tower and Air Traffic Services reporting office

children.text: The Approach Control Service is an air traffic control service

answers: An air traffic control service provided for the arriving and departing controlled flights
An air traffic control service for IFR flights arriving and departing
An air traffic control service provided for IFR and VFR flights within a Control Zone
An air traffic control service provided for IFR traffic within a Control Zone
correctAnswer: An air traffic control service provided for the arriving and departing controlled flights

children.text: Air traffic control service is provided for the purpose of:

answers: Avoiding colisions between all aircraft and maintaining an orderly flow of air traffic
Preventing collisions between controlled air traffic and expediting and maintaining an orderly flow of air traffic
Preventing collisions between aircraft, between aircraft and obstacles on the manoeuvring area and expediting and maintaining an orderly flow of air traffic
Applying separation between aircraft and expediting and maintaining an orderly flow of air traffic
correctAnswer: Preventing collisions between aircraft, between aircraft and obstacles on the manoeuvring area and expediting and maintaining an orderly flow of air traffic

children.text: To perform a VFR flight in airspace classification E /

answers: two way radiocommunication is not required
a clearance and/or two-way radiocommunication is required
a clearance and two-way radiocommunication is required
correctAnswer: two way radiocommunication is not required

children.text: Which statement is correct?

answers: The upper limit of a CTR shall be established at a height of at least 3000ft AMSL
The lower limit of a TMA shall be established at a height of at least 700ft AGL
The lower limit of an UIR may coincide with an IFR cruising level
The lower limit of a CTA shall be established at a height of at least 1500ft AGL
correctAnswer: The lower limit of a TMA shall be established at a height of at least 700ft AGL

children.text: The speed limitation for IFR flights inside ATS airspace classified as C, when flying below 3.050 m (10.000 ft) AMSL, is:
The speed limitation for VFR flights inside ATS airspace classified as C, when flying below 3.050 m (10.000 ft) AMSL, is:
The speed limitation for IFR flights inside ATS airspace classified as E, when flying below 3.050 m (10.000 ft) AMSL, is:
The speed limitation for both IFR flights and VFR flights inside ATS airspace classified as B, when flying below 3.050 m (10.000 ft) AMLS, is:
Where an upper flight information region (UIR) is established, the procedures applicable there in:

answers: need not to be identical with those applicable in the underlying flight information region
have to be as indicated by ICAO council
has to be the same as the underlying flight information region
have to be as agreed at the regional air navigation meetings
correctAnswer: need not to be identical with those applicable in the underlying flight information region

children.text: The VMC minima for a VFR flight inside an ATS airspace classified as B, is:

answers: 8 km visibility when at or above 3050 m (10.000 ft) AMSL and clear of clouds
5 MN visibility when below 3050 m (10.000 ft) AMSL, 1500 m horizontal and 300 m vertical from cloud
5 NM visibility below 3050 m (10.000 ft) AMSL, clear of clouds
8 km visibility when at or above 3050 m (10.000 ft) AMSL, and 1500 m horizontal and 300 m vertical from clouds
correctAnswer: 8 km visibility when at or above 3050 m (10.000 ft) AMSL, and 1500 m horizontal and 300 m vertical from clouds
explanation: QDB Information: Please observe: VMC minima, especially for airspace classification B: Swiss air law, rules of the air, article 38 distance from clouds: outside of clouds flight visibility at and over 3050m/10000ft AMSL 8km, below 3050m/10000ft AMSL 5km ICAO Annex 2 Rules of the air, table 3-1 distance from clouds: 1500m horizontally , 300m (1000ft) vertically flight visibility at and over 3050m/10000ft AMSL, 8km below 3050m AMSL 5km (Siehe PDF: Roules of the Air, Anhang)
